GHZ analyzer can be used to identify two of the $2^{N}$ maximally
entangled GHZ states
The GHZ analyzer can be utilized to identify two out of the $2^{N}$ maximally entangled GHZ states.
In our experiment, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:ghz}, $N = 3$, there are eight orthogonal GHZ states as follows.
In our experiment, as depicted in Fig. \ref{fig:ghz}, where $N = 3$, there exist eight orthogonal GHZ states presented below.
$\left|\Phi_0{ }^{\pm}\right\rangle$ states can be postselected according to the three-photon coincidence response. Note that the following relationship exists.
The $\left|\Phi_0{ }^{\pm}\right\rangle$ states can be postselected based on the three-photon coincidence response. It is worth noting that the following relationship holds.
the $|\Phi_0^{\pm}\rangle$ states below are postselected from the three-photon coincidence events
The $|\Phi_0^{\pm}\rangle$ states mentioned below are postselected from the three-photon coincidence events.

As mentioned in the main text, free-space optical elements used in the GHZ-state analyzer brings advantages. First, the calibration of polarization reference-frame will be more convenient, including polarization alignment between the photon sources (Alice, Bob and Charlie) and GHZ-state analyzer (Service node, Eve), X basis measurement in GHZ-state analyzer based on fiber polarization controller (FPC) and fiber polarization beam splitter (FPBS). Second, a quater wave plate (QWP) and an half wave plate (HWP) in each mode can be used to compensate the reflection phase introduced by PBS  and the transmission phase of X base quantum state introduced by fiber spool simultaneously. Finally, free-space PBS has a higher extinction ratio than fiber PBS, which helps reduce the quantum bits error rate (QBER) of Z basis and improve the key rate.

As mentioned in the main text, the utilization of free-space optical elements in the GHZ-state analyzer offers several advantages. Firstly, it facilitates the calibration of the polarization reference frame, including the alignment of polarization between the photon sources (Alice, Bob, and Charlie) and the GHZ-state analyzer (Service node, Eve), X basis measurement in the GHZ-state analyzer based on fiber polarization controller (FPC), and fiber polarization beam splitter (FPBS). Secondly, the introduction of a quarter-wave plate (QWP) and a half-wave plate (HWP) in each mode enables compensation for the reflection phase introduced by PBS and the transmission phase of X base quantum state introduced by the fiber spool simultaneously. Lastly, free-space PBS exhibits a higher extinction ratio compared to fiber PBS, which helps reduce the quantum bit error rate (QBER) of the Z basis and improve the key rate.

As mentioned in the main text, the utilization of free-space optical elements in the GHZ analyzer offers several advantages. Firstly, it facilitates the calibration of the polarization reference frame, including the alignment of polarization between the photon sources (Alice, Bob, and Charlie) and the GHZ analyzer (Service node, Eve), X basis measurement in the GHZ analyzer based on fiber polarization controller (FPC), and fiber polarization beam splitter (FPBS). Secondly, the introduction of a quarter-wave plate (QWP) and a half-wave plate (HWP) in each mode enables compensation for the reflection phase introduced by PBS and the transmission phase of X base quantum state introduced by the fiber spool simultaneously. Lastly, free-space PBS exhibits a higher extinction ratio compared to fiber PBS, which helps reduce the quantum bit error rate (QBER) of the Z basis and improve the key rate.

As discussed in the main text, the utilization of free-space optical elements in the GHZ analyzer offers several notable advantages. Firstly, it simplifies the calibration process of the polarization reference frame. This includes streamlining the alignment of polarization between the photon sources (Alice, Bob, and Charlie) and the GHZ analyzer (Service node, Eve), as well as facilitating X basis measurements in the GHZ analyzer through the use of fiber polarization controller (FPC) and fiber polarization beam splitter (FPBS). Secondly, the inclusion of a quarter-wave plate (QWP) and a half-wave plate (HWP) in each mode allows for simultaneous compensation of the reflection phase introduced by PBS and the transmission phase of the X base quantum state introduced by the fiber spool. Lastly, free-space PBS exhibits a higher extinction ratio compared to fiber PBS, resulting in a reduction of the quantum bit error rate (QBER) in the Z basis, ultimately facilitating an improvement in the overall key rate.

In this work, we experimentally demonstrate the first MDI-QCKA over a 60 km fiber link by developing independent light source three-photon interference technology and a secret rate of 45.5 bit/s is obtained. In the experiment, Alice, Bob and Charlie use the same parameters because of symmetric channel loss, we denote $\mu_{2}$, $\mu_{1}$, $\mu_{0}$ to be the intensity of signal state, decoy state, vacuum state respectively, $P^{Z(X)}_{\mu}$ to be the probability of Z(X) basis for corresponding intensity $\mu$. Experimental parameters are shown in TABLE \ref{tab:parameters}

In this work, we experimentally demonstrate the first MDI-QCKA over a 60 km fiber link by developing independent light source three-photon interference technology. The achieved secret rate is 45.5 bit/s. In the experiment, Alice, Bob, and Charlie use the same parameters due to symmetric channel loss. We denote $\mu_{2}$, $\mu_{1}$, and $\mu_{0}$ as the intensity of the signal state, decoy state, and vacuum state, respectively. $P^{Z(X)}_{\mu}$ represents the probability of Z(X) basis for the corresponding intensity $\mu$. The experimental parameters are shown in TABLE \ref{tab:parameters}.

In this work, we experimentally demonstrate the first MDI-QCKA over a 60 km fiber link by developing independent light source three-photon interference technology and a secret rate of 45.5 bit/s is obtained.

In this work, we experimentally demonstrate the first MDI-QCKA over a 60 km fiber link by developing independent light source three-photon interference technology. The obtained secret rate is 45.5 bit/s.

In the experiment, Alice, Bob and Charlie use the same parameters because of symmetric channel loss, we denote $\mu_{2}$, $\mu_{1}$, $\mu_{0}$ to be the intensity of signal state, decoy state, vacuum state respectively, $P^{Z(X)}_{\mu}$ to be the probability of Z(X) basis for corresponding intensity $\mu$. Experimental parameters are shown in TABLE \ref{tab:parameters}

During the experiment, Alice, Bob, and Charlie utilize the same parameters due to symmetric channel loss. We designate $\mu_{2}$, $\mu_{1}$, and $\mu_{0}$ as the intensities of the signal state, decoy state, and vacuum state respectively. Additionally, $P^{Z(X)}_{\mu}$ represents the probability of the Z(X) basis for the corresponding intensity $\mu$. The experimental parameters are presented in TABLE \ref{tab:parameters}.
